title: Database Architecture Review — Performance & Cost Improvements
summary: 12 recommendations across scraper DB (DB-001..006), pipeline DB (DB-007..009), and cross-cutting architecture (DB-010..012). The #1 issue remains view-based full table scans on the scraper DB, which also blocks single-KRS pipeline triggers (the pipeline reads krs_documents_current from the scraper DB). The pipeline DB inherits the same ROW_NUMBER() view anti-pattern and should be fixed before launch.

architecture_context.dual_database_design: Scraper DB (rdf-postgres) is read-only for the pipeline. Pipeline DB (rdf-pipeline) owns financial data, features, models, predictions, and orchestration. BigQuery (rdf_analytics) handles batch analytics and population stats, synced back to PG for the API.

two_execution_paths.batch: Cloud Run Job on schedule. Discovers changed docs since last run, processes in bulk. BQ sync + population stats computed at end.
two_execution_paths.single_krs: User triggers /api/pipeline/queue + /api/pipeline/trigger for one KRS. Full pipeline: download → parse → features → scoring. Must be fast (<30s) for interactive use. This path reads krs_documents_current from scraper DB (currently full table scan).

data_flow: 2. ETL: scraper_db.krs_documents_current (storage_path) → parse XML → pipeline_db.financial_line_items (COPY bulk)
7. Serve: API reads pipeline_db for predictions + peer-stats

recommendations.title: Fix random_page_cost for SSD storage
recommendations.problem: Cloud SQL uses SSD storage but random_page_cost is set to 4.0 (spinning disk default). This makes the PostgreSQL planner prefer sequential scans over index scans even when indexes would be much faster. It directly contributes to the full table scan problem.

steps.note: Takes effect for new connections. Existing connections keep old value until they reconnect.

steps.sql: SELECT name, setting FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'random_page_cost';

recommendations.title: Simplify _current views to use WHERE is_current = true directly
recommendations.impact: HIGHEST — eliminates 244M sequential tuple reads. Also critical for single-KRS pipeline triggers that read krs_documents_current from scraper DB.
recommendations.problem: The krs_documents_current and krs_entities_current views use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY document_id ORDER BY version_no DESC) to find the latest version. But callers filter by krs. PostgreSQL CANNOT push the WHERE krs = ... filter down through the window function — it must scan ALL 1.9M rows, compute rankings, THEN discard non-matching rows. This is the root cause of 244M sequential tuple reads on krs_document_versions. The is_current column already tracks the same information, maintained by the application on every write. The pipeline's single-KRS trigger also reads krs_documents_current to look up storage_path — this full table scan makes interactive triggers unacceptably slow.

plan: Subquery Scan on ranked (filter: rn = 1 AND krs = '0000694720')
-> Parallel Seq Scan on krs_document_versions (1.9M rows)
root_cause_explain_plan.problem_detail: The window function forces a full table scan + sort of 850K current rows before the krs filter can be applied. For a single KRS lookup this scans ~460K rows to return ~3.

files_to_modify.current_view_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW krs_documents_current AS SELECT document_id, krs, rodzaj, status, ... FROM (SELECT kdv.*, row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY kdv.document_id ORDER BY kdv.version_no DESC, kdv.version_id DESC) AS rn FROM krs_document_versions kdv WHERE kdv.is_current = true) ranked WHERE rn = 1
files_to_modify.new_view_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW krs_documents_current AS SELECT document_id, krs, rodzaj, status, nazwa, okres_start, okres_end, filename, is_ifrs, is_correction, date_filed, date_prepared, is_downloaded, downloaded_at, storage_path, storage_backend, file_size_bytes, zip_size_bytes, file_count, file_types, discovered_at, metadata_fetched_at, download_error FROM krs_document_versions WHERE is_current = true
files_to_modify.why: Since the application already maintains is_current = true on exactly one row per document_id (and sets is_current = false on superseded rows), the ROW_NUMBER() window function is redundant. Removing it lets PostgreSQL push WHERE krs = ... filters into the base table scan, using the idx_krs_doc_versions_krs_current index.

files_to_modify.current_view_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW krs_entities_current AS SELECT krs, name, legal_form, status, ... FROM (SELECT kev.*, row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY kev.krs ORDER BY kev.valid_from DESC, kev.version_id DESC) AS rn FROM krs_entity_versions kev WHERE kev.is_current = true) ranked WHERE rn = 1
files_to_modify.new_view_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW krs_entities_current AS SELECT krs, name, legal_form, status, registered_at, last_changed_at, nip, regon, address_city, address_street, address_postal_code, raw, source, valid_from AS synced_at FROM krs_entity_versions WHERE is_current = true
files_to_modify.why: Same reasoning as documents. The application already ensures exactly one is_current = true row per krs via BEGIN/UPDATE/INSERT/COMMIT blocks in upsert_entity_version().

before_deploying: Verify is_current invariant holds in production: SELECT document_id, COUNT(*) AS c FROM krs_document_versions WHERE is_current = true GROUP BY document_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
Same for entities: SELECT krs, COUNT(*) AS c FROM krs_entity_versions WHERE is_current = true GROUP BY krs HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
Both queries should return 0 rows. If they return rows, the is_current flag has duplicates and must be fixed before simplifying the view.
after_deploying: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM krs_documents_current WHERE krs = '0000694720';
The plan should show an Index Scan on idx_krs_doc_versions_krs_current, NOT a Parallel Seq Scan.

implementation.rollback: Revert to the original view definitions with ROW_NUMBER().

recommendations.title: Drop legacy tables (krs_entities, krs_documents) — saves 514 MB
recommendations.risk: MEDIUM — must verify no direct reads
recommendations.problem: The legacy krs_entities (253 MB) and krs_documents (261 MB) tables are write-duplicates of the versioned tables. Every batch worker insert writes to BOTH the versioned table and the legacy table. All read paths already use the _current views on the versioned tables. The legacy tables waste 514 MB of storage and double the write I/O.

steps.description: Verify legacy tables have no direct reads in production
steps.sql: SELECT relname, seq_scan + idx_scan AS total_scans, seq_tup_read + idx_tup_fetch AS total_rows_read FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname IN ('krs_entities', 'krs_documents');
steps.note: If total_scans is significant, identify the callers before proceeding. Check all code paths that reference these table names directly (not through views).

steps.description: Search codebase for direct references to legacy tables
steps.note: Also run: grep -rn 'krs_documents\b' app/ batch/ --include='*.py' | grep -v 'krs_document_versions' | grep -v '_current'. These show all code paths that INSERT/UPDATE/SELECT the legacy tables directly.

steps.description: Remove legacy table writes from application code
files: app/repositories/krs_repo.py — remove INSERT INTO krs_entities ON CONFLICT in upsert_entity_version()
app/scraper/db.py — remove INSERT INTO krs_documents ON CONFLICT in insert_documents()
batch/entity_store.py — remove INSERT INTO krs_entities ON CONFLICT in save_entity()
batch/rdf_document_store.py — remove INSERT INTO krs_documents ON CONFLICT and UPDATE krs_documents in save_document()

steps.description: Remove legacy table CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS from schema init
files: app/repositories/krs_repo.py — remove CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS krs_entities
app/scraper/db.py — remove CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS krs_documents
batch/entity_store.py — remove CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS krs_entities
batch/rdf_document_store.py — remove CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS krs_documents

steps.description: Deploy code changes, then drop legacy tables in production
sql: -- pg_dump -h HOST -U postgres -d rdf -t krs_entities -t krs_documents > legacy_backup.sql
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS krs_documents CASCADE;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS krs_entities CASCADE;

implementation.rollback: Restore from the pg_dump backup taken in step 5.
implementation.savings: 514 MB storage + ~2x write throughput improvement for batch workers

recommendations.title: Drop unused indexes — saves 39 MB + faster writes
recommendations.problem: 39 MB of indexes have had zero scans since last stats reset. Every INSERT into the parent table must also update these indexes, wasting CPU and I/O on the batch workers.

steps.description: Drop the large unused indexes (entity versioning)
sql: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_krs_entity_versions_current;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_krs_entity_versions_valid_from;

steps.description: Also remove the CREATE INDEX statements from the code so they don't get recreated on next deploy
files: app/repositories/krs_repo.py — remove idx_krs_entity_versions_current and idx_krs_entity_versions_valid_from from _init_schema()

steps.description: Optionally drop small unused indexes on prediction tables (scraper DB)
sql: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_features_krs;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_features_year;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_reports_krs;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_reports_year;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_reports_logical;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_companies_pkd;
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_companies_nip;
steps.note: These tables are tiny (<100 rows) on the scraper DB. The pipeline DB (rdf-pipeline) will have its own copies of these indexes where they WILL be used. Safe to drop on the scraper DB.

implementation.rollback: Recreate the indexes with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

recommendations.title: Add partial indexes for current-row lookups
recommendations.problem: After simplifying the views (DB-002), queries will use WHERE is_current = true AND krs = .... The existing index idx_krs_doc_versions_krs_current covers (krs, is_current) but includes all rows. A partial index on krs WHERE is_current = true only indexes the ~857K current rows instead of all 1.9M, making it smaller and faster.

steps.description: Create partial indexes for current-row lookups
sql: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_doc_versions_current_krs ON krs_document_versions (krs) WHERE is_current = true;
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_entity_versions_current_krs ON krs_entity_versions (krs) WHERE is_current = true;

steps.description: Drop the old non-partial index that this replaces
sql: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_krs_doc_versions_krs_current;
steps.savings: 25 MB (replaced by a ~12 MB partial index)

steps.description: Update code to match — replace old index name in _init_schema()
files: app/scraper/db.py — replace idx_krs_doc_versions_krs_current definition with the new partial index
app/repositories/krs_repo.py — add idx_entity_versions_current_krs partial index to _init_schema()

implementation.verification: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM krs_documents_current WHERE krs = '0000694720'; — should show Index Scan using idx_doc_versions_current_krs

recommendations.title: Raise work_mem to 16 MB
recommendations.problem: The prediction views (latest_financial_reports, latest_computed_features, latest_financial_line_items) use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) which requires sorting. At 4 MB work_mem, sorts on tables with >1000 rows spill to disk. Raising to 16 MB keeps most sorts in RAM.

steps.note: Worst case memory: max_connections (100) * work_mem (16 MB) = 1.6 GB. This is fine for db-g1-small (1.7 GB RAM) but exceeds db-f1-micro (614 MB). If staying on db-f1-micro, use 8 MB instead.

implementation.note: If the instance is db-f1-micro, consider upgrading to db-g1-small (~$25/month) before applying this.

recommendations.title: Simplify pipeline DB latest_* views — prevent inheriting the scraper DB full-scan problem
recommendations.impact: HIGH (preventive — avoids a repeat of the 244M seq read disaster)
recommendations.risk: ZERO (pipeline DB is new, no production data yet)
recommendations.problem: The pipeline DB (app/db/pipeline_db.py) creates identical latest_* views using ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY version DESC). The pipeline uses DELETE + re-INSERT at version=1 for idempotency, so there is ALWAYS exactly one row per natural key. The window function is purely defensive and will cause the same full-table-scan problem when the pipeline DB grows to production scale. Since the pipeline DB is new and has no production data, this is the ideal time to fix the views before any data lands.

files_to_modify.what: Replace all 4 latest_* view definitions with simple WHERE clauses

changes.current: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY logical_key ORDER BY report_version DESC, created_at DESC, id DESC)
changes.new_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW latest_financial_reports AS SELECT id, logical_key, report_version, supersedes_report_id, krs, data_source_id, report_type, fiscal_year, period_start, period_end, taxonomy_version, source_document_id, source_file_path, ingestion_status, ingestion_error, created_at, schema_code FROM financial_reports fr WHERE report_version = (SELECT MAX(fr2.report_version) FROM financial_reports fr2 WHERE fr2.logical_key = fr.logical_key)
changes.why: Correlated subquery lets PostgreSQL use the (logical_key, report_version) UNIQUE index for each row, avoiding the full-table window sort. For single-KRS lookups the planner can push krs filter into the outer query.

changes.current: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY report_id, section, tag_path ORDER BY extraction_version DESC)
changes.new_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW latest_financial_line_items AS SELECT report_id, section, tag_path, extraction_version, label_pl, value_current, value_previous, currency, schema_code FROM financial_line_items WHERE extraction_version = 1
changes.why: The pipeline always DELETEs extraction_version=1 then re-inserts at extraction_version=1. There is never >1 version. A simple WHERE extraction_version = 1 replaces the window function with zero overhead.

changes.current: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY report_id, feature_definition_id ORDER BY computation_version DESC, computed_at DESC)
changes.new_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW latest_computed_features AS SELECT report_id, feature_definition_id, krs, fiscal_year, value, is_valid, error_message, source_extraction_version, computation_version, computed_at FROM computed_features WHERE computation_version = 1
changes.why: Same as line_items: pipeline always DELETEs computation_version=1 then re-inserts. Single version in practice.

changes.current: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY logical_key ORDER BY report_version DESC, created_at DESC, id DESC) with WHERE ingestion_status = 'completed'
changes.new_sql: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW latest_successful_financial_reports AS SELECT id, logical_key, report_version, supersedes_report_id, krs, data_source_id, report_type, fiscal_year, period_start, period_end, taxonomy_version, source_document_id, source_file_path, ingestion_status, ingestion_error, created_at, schema_code FROM financial_reports fr WHERE ingestion_status = 'completed' AND report_version = (SELECT MAX(fr2.report_version) FROM financial_reports fr2 WHERE fr2.logical_key = fr.logical_key AND fr2.ingestion_status = 'completed')
changes.why: Same pattern as latest_financial_reports but filtered to completed only.

implementation.note: If future requirements introduce multi-version retention (extraction_version > 1), add an is_latest column maintained by the pipeline writer rather than reverting to window functions.

recommendations.title: Add pipeline_queue cleanup policy + index for discovery query
recommendations.impact: MEDIUM (prevents queue table bloat over time)
recommendations.problem: The pipeline_queue table accumulates completed/failed rows indefinitely. After months of batch runs processing thousands of KRS, the table will bloat. The queue also lacks an index for the discovery query that joins krs_document_versions from the scraper DB. Additionally, the FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED claim pattern needs the pending partial index to be efficient.

files_to_modify.what: Add cleanup and missing indexes to _init_schema()

additions.description: Add a completed_at index for periodic cleanup queries
additions.sql: CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_pipeline_queue_completed ON pipeline_queue (completed_at) WHERE status IN ('completed', 'failed')

additions.description: Add a composite index for the claim query (FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on pending items)
additions.sql: CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_pipeline_queue_claim ON pipeline_queue (status, queued_at) WHERE status = 'pending'
additions.note: The existing idx_pipeline_queue_pending is (status) WHERE status = 'pending'. Adding queued_at to the index lets the claim query use index-only scan for FIFO ordering.

files_to_modify.what: Add a cleanup function to purge old completed items
files_to_modify.new_function: def cleanup_completed(conn, older_than_days=30): conn.execute('DELETE FROM pipeline_queue WHERE status IN (%s, %s) AND completed_at < now() - interval %s', ['completed', 'failed', f'{older_than_days} days'])
files_to_modify.note: Call this at the end of each pipeline run or on a separate schedule.

recommendations.title: Add BQ deduplication for incremental WRITE_APPEND tables
recommendations.impact: MEDIUM (prevents duplicate rows in BQ analytics)
recommendations.problem: The BQ sync uses WRITE_APPEND for line_items, computed_features, and predictions. If the same KRS is processed twice (re-queue, retry, or correction), duplicate rows land in BQ. Over time this inflates BQ storage costs and skews analytics (e.g., population_stats double-counts companies).

options.description: Change bq_sync.py to use a staging table + MERGE INTO pattern instead of direct WRITE_APPEND
pattern: 1. Load Parquet into a temp/staging table (WRITE_TRUNCATE)
2. MERGE INTO target FROM staging ON natural_key WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT
options.note: MERGE is the idiomatic BQ pattern for idempotent loads. It prevents duplicates at ingestion time.

options.description: Create BQ views that deduplicate using ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY natural_key ORDER BY exported_at DESC)
options.note: Simpler to implement but wastes BQ storage and compute. The underlying tables still have duplicates. Only use this if the MERGE approach is too complex to implement immediately.

recommendations.title: Add proper primary key and index to population_stats
recommendations.impact: MEDIUM (fast API lookups for peer-stats endpoint)
recommendations.problem: The population_stats table is queried by the /api/pipeline/peer-stats/{krs} endpoint which looks up (pkd_code, tenure_bucket, model_id). The table is TRUNCATE+COPY refreshed from BQ so it has no primary key constraint. Without a proper index, the API endpoint does a sequential scan of the entire table for each request.

files_to_modify.what: Add a unique index after COPY (or define PK in CREATE TABLE)
files_to_modify.sql: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_population_stats_lookup ON population_stats (pkd_code, tenure_bucket, model_id)
files_to_modify.note: Since the table is TRUNCATE+COPYd, indexes are rebuilt from scratch each refresh. A unique index on (pkd_code, tenure_bucket, model_id) ensures the API lookup is an index scan, not a seq scan. The UNIQUE constraint also prevents duplicate BQ rows from sneaking in.

recommendations.title: Apply random_page_cost = 1.1 on pipeline DB at creation time
recommendations.problem: The pipeline DB will be a new Cloud SQL instance, also on SSD. The same random_page_cost=4.0 default will apply. Set it correctly from the start.

steps.description: Set random_page_cost on pipeline DB at creation
Also set work_mem appropriately from the start
steps.note: The pipeline DB will have fewer max_connections (the Cloud Run Job is a single process). 16 MB work_mem is safe.

implementation.note: Add these to the pipeline DB setup runbook (docs/PIPELINE_GCP_SETUP.md) so they're applied on fresh instances.

recommendations.title: Add covering index on scraper DB for pipeline discovery query
recommendations.impact: MEDIUM (speeds up batch pipeline discovery phase)
recommendations.problem: The pipeline discovery phase (pipeline/queue.py::enqueue_changed_since) queries krs_document_versions from the scraper DB with WHERE observed_at > %s (or valid_from > %s) to find recently changed documents. Without an index on the timestamp column used for discovery, this is a sequential scan of the 1.9M row table. The existing idx_krs_doc_versions_valid_from index has 0 scans currently but will be used by the pipeline.

steps.description: Check which timestamp column the pipeline discovery uses
steps.note: The discovery query likely filters on observed_at or valid_from. Verify the column name before creating the index.

steps.description: Create a covering index for the discovery query
sql_options: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_doc_versions_observed ON krs_document_versions (observed_at DESC) INCLUDE (krs, document_id) WHERE is_current = true;
-- idx_krs_doc_versions_valid_from already exists (currently unused), so just add INCLUDE columns:
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_krs_doc_versions_valid_from;
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_doc_versions_valid_from_covering ON krs_document_versions (valid_from DESC) INCLUDE (krs, document_id) WHERE is_current = true;
steps.note: The INCLUDE (krs, document_id) makes this a covering index — the discovery query can be satisfied from the index alone without heap access. The WHERE is_current = true partial filter keeps the index small (~850K rows instead of 1.9M).

phase_1.name: Quick wins on scraper DB (deploy immediately)
phase_1.description: Set random_page_cost = 1.1 and drop unused indexes. Zero risk, immediate benefit.

phase_2.name: View simplification on scraper DB (validate then deploy)
phase_2.description: Simplify _current views, add partial indexes, and add discovery covering index. Must validate is_current invariant before deploying. DB-012 prepares for pipeline discovery reads.
phase_2.total_savings: Eliminates ~244M sequential tuple reads + 13 MB index savings + fast single-KRS pipeline triggers

phase_3.name: Legacy table removal on scraper DB (after validation period)
phase_3.description: Remove legacy table writes from code, deploy, then drop tables. Run for 1 week with writes removed but tables still present to validate nothing breaks.

phase_4.name: Tuning on scraper DB
phase_4.description: Raise work_mem. Consider upgrading to db-g1-small if staying on f1-micro is too tight.

phase_5.name: Pipeline DB setup (before etl_pipeline merge)
phase_5.description: Apply all pipeline DB fixes BEFORE merging etl_pipeline to main. These are zero-risk changes to code that has no production data yet. Prevents inheriting the scraper DB's architectural mistakes.
phase_5.note: DB-007 (simplified views) and DB-011 (SSD planner settings) should be applied in the etl_pipeline branch before merge.

phase_6.name: BigQuery dedup (after first batch run)
phase_6.description: Switch BQ sync from WRITE_APPEND to MERGE-based dedup. Can be deferred until after the first successful batch run, but should be done before re-processing any KRS.

total_projected_savings.scraper_db_storage: ~566 MB (DB shrinks from 2.0 GB to ~1.4 GB)
total_projected_savings.sequential_reads: ~244M tuple reads eliminated per stats cycle
total_projected_savings.write_throughput: ~2x for batch workers (fewer indexes + no legacy table duplication)
total_projected_savings.single_krs_pipeline_trigger: From ~5-10s (full table scan) to ~10ms (index scan) for document lookup
total_projected_savings.pipeline_db_prevention: Avoids repeating the scraper DB's 244M seq read problem on a new database
total_projected_savings.monthly_cost: Extends db-f1-micro viability by ~5-10x growth, delaying $17/month upgrade
